DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Oct-07-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Lori K. Komshian, Esq. of McNamara, Ney, Beatty, Slattery, Borges & Ambagher LLP, T:925.939.5330, for defendant and moving party;
Charles P. Stone, Esq. of the Law Offices of Katherine Moore, T:650.995.7312, for plaintiff and opposing party.

RULING: The tentative ruling was contested and argued. At the conclusion of arguments, the Court adopted the tentative ruling on DEFENDANT PAUL YEP DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT, as follows:

Defendant Paul Yep's demurrer to Plaintiff Timothy Hansen's first amended complaint is sustained without leave to amend as to the third cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress. A viable claim for intentional infliction of emotional distress requires "outrageous conduct" on behalf of the defendant. (Davidson v. City of Westminster (1982) 32 Cal.3d 197, 209.) For conduct to be outrageous, it must be "so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized community." (Id.) "The tort calls for intentional, or at least reckless conduct - conduct intended to inflict injury or engaged in with the realization that injury will result." (Id. at 210.) "[I]t it generally held that there can be no recovery for mere profanity, obscenity, or abuse, without circumstances of aggravation, or for insults, indignities or threats which are considered to amount to nothing more than mere annoyances." (Yurick v. Superior Court (1989) 209 Cal.App.3d 1116, 1128; see also Kruse v. Bank of America (1988) 202 Cal.App.3d 38, 68 ["heartless and insensitive remarks [may] merit opprobrium, [but] they do not qualify as the kind of 'outrageous' conduct necessary to support an action for intentional infliction of emotional distress"].)

Here, Defendant's conduct as pled does not constitute outrageous conduct as a matter of law. Defendant's alleged evasiveness, statement that he is a police officer while throwing down his identification, and driving past the Hansen household while staring in a "menacing fashion," are annoyances, but do not rise to the level of extreme and intolerable behavior. (1st Am. Compl. 5, 26-32.) Although an allegation of intoxication while driving a motor vehicle is regarded with dismay, the alleged intoxication is not outrageous and "so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized community." (Davidson v. City of Westminster, 32 Cal.3d at 209.) Nor does Defendant's alleged conduct rise to the level of intimidation under the color of authority. (Cf., e.g., Tekle v. U.S. (9th Cir. 2007) 511 F.3d 839, 856 [outrageous conduct found where the officer kept their weapons pointed at the plaintiff, picked him off the ground by the handcuffs, made disparaging comments, and spit on him]; Crain v. Krehiel (N.D.Cal. 1977) 443 F.Supp. 202, 212 [agents' alleged threat to "burn" plaintiff in court could reasonably be construed as a threat to expose informant and was therefore considered outrageous].)

Order signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Punitive Damages From Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Oct-07-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Lori K. Komshian, Esq. of McNamara, Ney, Beatty, Slattery, Borges & Ambagher LLP, T:925.939.5330, for defendant and moving party;
Charles P. Stone, Esq. of the Law Offices of Katherine Moore, T:650.995.7312, for plaintiff and opposing party.

RULING: The tentative ruling was contested and argued. At the conclusion of arguments, the Court adopted the tentative ruling on DEFENDANT PAUL YEP's MOTION TO STRIKE PUNITIVE DAMAGES AND MALICE ALLEGATIONS FROM PLAINTIFFS' FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT, as follows:

Defendant Paul Yep's motion to strike punitive damages and malice allegations from Plaintiffs Timothy Hansen and Erin Hansen's complaint is denied.

The allegations in the complaint support a prayer of punitive damages. Civil Code section 3294(a) limits punitive damages to circumstances "where it is proven by clear and convincing evidence that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice." Punitive damages may not be pled with conclusory allegations; the plaintiff must plead facts to support the request. (See G.D. Searle & Co. v. Superior Court (1975) 49 Cal.App.3d 22, 29 ["Notwithstanding relaxed pleading criteria, certain tortious injuries demand firm allegations. Vague, conclusory allegations . . . may not be rescued by the rule of liberal construction."].)

As explained in the Court's ruling on Defendant's demurrer, there is no basis for a cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress. However, the Court finds there are other specifically pled facts to support a finding of malice. The first amended complaint alleges Defendant smelled of alcohol, his speech was slurred, and he had an unsteady gait. (1st Am. Compl. 6, 12.) Furthermore, Defendant is a police officer and Plaintiff alleges Defendant was drinking before the accident occurred, knew the illegality of operating a motor vehicle while under the influence of alcohol, was aware of the danger, and acted with conscious disregard of this danger and the safety of others. (Id. 12-13.) "One who willfully consumes alcoholic beverages to the point of intoxication, knowing that he thereafter must operate a motor vehicle, thereby combining sharply impaired physical and mental faculties with a vehicle capable of great force and speed, reasonably may be held to exhibit a conscious disregard of the safety of others." (Taylor v. Superior Court (1979) 24 Cal.3d 890, 897; see also, e.g., Dawes v. Superior Court (1980) 111 Cal.App.3d 82, 88 [Taylor held that "driving a vehicle while intoxicated may in appropriate circumstances evidence a conscious disregard of probable injury to others and be sufficient to warrant an award of punitive damages."] [holding that trial court improperly struck allegations of conduct by allegedly intoxicated driver that would support a recovery of punitive damages].) Plaintiffs sufficiently allege that Defendant was aware of the probable dangerous consequences of his conduct and that he deliberately failed to avoid those consequences. (See Taylor v. Superior Court, 24 Cal.3d at 856; Civ. Code, 3294, subd. (c)(1).)

Order signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com

Notice Of Hearing On Motion To Strike Punitive Damages And Alcohol Allegations From Plaintiffs Complaint
Off Calendar Jun-19-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Charles Stone, esq. of Law Offices of Katherine Moore for Plaintiff.
Kirsten L. Sansoe, esq. (925-939-5330) of McNamara, Ney, Beatty, ...LLP for Defendant via Court Call.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

Defendant Paul Yep's motion to strike punitive damages and alcohol allegations from Plaintiffs Timothy Hansen and Erin Hansen's complaint is granted. Paragraph 6, the second sentence of paragraph 12, and the prayer for punitive damages are stricken with leave to amend from the complaint.

The allegations in the complaint do not support a prayer for punitive damages. Civil Code section 3294(a) limits punitive damages to circumstances "where it is proven by clear and convincing evidence that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice." Punitive damages may not be pled with conclusory allegations; Plaintiff must plead facts to support the request. (See G.D. Searle & Co. v. Superior Court (1975) 49 Cal.App.3d 22, 29 ["Notwithstanding relaxed pleading criteria, certain tortious injuries demand firm allegations. Vague, conclusory allegations . . . may not be rescued by the rule of liberal construction."].) As explained in the Court's ruling on Defendant's demurrer, there is no basis for a cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress. And there are no other specifically pled facts to support a finding of oppression, fraud, or malice.

The allegations in paragraphs 6 and 12 on information and belief that Defendant had been drinking and was under the influence of alcohol are conclusory and lack basis in ultimate facts. The complaint does not allege that Defendant was slurring his words, had difficulty standing, or exhibited other symptoms of alcohol consumption. Rather, it merely alleges that he caused a car accident and subsequently behaved in an evasive manner when asked to provide identification and insurance information. That is not a sufficient basis to make these allegations, nor are they sufficient to support a prayer for punitive damages.

Defendant to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-19-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Charles Stone, esq. of Law Offices of Katherine Moore for Plaintiff.
Kirsten L. Sansoe, esq. (925-939-5330) of McNamara, Ney, Beatty, ...LLP for Defendant via Court Call.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

Defendant Paul Yep's demurrer to Plaintiff Timothy Hansen's complaint is sustained with leave to amend as to the third cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress. A viable claim for intentional infliction of emotional distress requires "outrageous conduct" on behalf of the defendant. (Davidson v. City of Westminster (1982) 32 Cal.3d 197, 209.) For conduct to be outrageous, it must be "so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized community." (Id.) "[I]t is generally held that there can be no recovery for mere profanity, obscenity, or abuse, without circumstances of aggravation, or for insults, indignities or threats which are considered to amount to nothing more than mere annoyances." (Yurick v. Superior Court (1989) 209 Cal.App.3d 1116, 1128.) Defendant's conduct as pled does not constitute outrageous conduct as a matter of law. Even liberally construing the complaint, Defendant's alleged evasiveness, his statement that he is a police officer while throwing down his identification, and driving past the Hansen household while staring in a "menacing fashion," are annoyances, but do not rise to the level of extreme and intolerable behavior. (See id.; Davidson, 32 Cal.3d at 209; Compl. 5, 26-31.)

Molko v. Holy Spirit Assn. (1988) 46 Cal.3d 1092, 1102-1106, 1122 involved very different circumstances of a church abusing its authority by fraudulently coercing the plaintiffs into joining and serving the church. By contrast, Defendant's statements and behavior here, while allegedly unnerving, were not fraudulent or patently threatening in such a way as to rise to the level of outrageous conduct. Plaintiff has not indicated that any amendment to the complaint would support a finding of outrageous conduct.(so leave to amend the complaint is not appropriate here.)***20 days to amend.

Defendant to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
